SUMMARY:
## Algorithmic Bias in AI Employment Decisions Throughout each step of the employment search process, AI tools fall short in accurately capturing candidate potential capabilities. Given that the potential for bias is embedded throughout the employment search process, legal scholars speculate courts are more likely to scrutinize discriminatory outcomes under the disparate impact theory of discrimination. With the possibility of bias embedded throughout each step of the recruiting process, employers must do their part to gather information about the performance of the AI system they ultimately invest in. To effectively mitigate unintentional bias in AI-driven hiring, stakeholders, ranging from HR professionals to developers and candidates, must understand the technology’s limitations, ensure its job-related decision-making accuracy, and promote transparent, informed use, while also maintaining robust DEI initiatives and awareness of candidates’ rights.

# JTIP Blog

## Algorithmic Bias in AI Employment Decisions

Zara Siddiqui 
|
January 30, 2025

Algorithmic bias is AI’s Achilles heel, revealing how machines are only as unbiased as the humans behind them.

The most prevalent real-world stage for human versus machine bias is the job search process. What started out as newspaper ads and flyers at local coffee shops, is now a completely digital process with click-through ads, interactive chatbots, resume data translation, and computer-screened candidate interviews.

Artificial intelligence encompasses a wide variety of tools, but in context to HR specifically, common AI tools include Machine Learning algorithms that conduct complex and layered statical analysis modeling human cognition (neural networks), computer vision that classifies and labels content on images or video, and large language models.

AI-enabled employment tools are powerful gatekeepers that determine the future of natural persons. With over 70% of companies using this technology investing into the promise of efficiency and neutrality, these abilities have recently come into question as these technologies have the potential to discriminate against protected classes.

**Anecdote**

On February 20, 2024, Plaintiff Derek Mobley initiated a class action lawsuit against an AI-enabled HR organization WorkDay, Inc., [for engaging in a “pattern and practice” of discrimination based on race, age, and disability in violation of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Civil Rights Act of 1886, Age Discrimination Act of 1967, and ADA Amendments Act of 2008.](https://fingfx.thomsonreuters.com/gfx/legaldocs/xmvjrbanovr/EMPLOYMENT_WORKDAY_COMPLAINT_complaint.pdf) WorkDay Inc., according to the complaint, disproportionately disqualifies African-Americans, individuals over the age of 40, and individuals with disabilities securing gainful employment.

WorkDay provides subscription-based AI HR solutions to medium and large sized firms in a variety of industries. The system screens candidates based on human inputs and algorithms and according to the complaint, WorkDay employs an automated system, in lieu of human judgement, to determine how high volume of applicants should be processed on behalf of their business clients.

The plaintiff and members of the class have applied for numerous jobs that use WorkDay’s platforms and received several rejections. This process has deterred the plaintiff and members of the class from applying to companies that use WorkDay’s platform.

**Legal History of AI Employment Discrimination**

Mobley vs. WorkDay is the first-class action lawsuit against an AI solution company for employment discrimination, but this is not the first time that an AI organization is being sued for employment discrimination.

In August 2023, the EEOC settled the first-of-its-kind Employment Discrimination lawsuit against a virtual tutoring company that programmed its recruitment software to automatically reject older candidates. The company was required to pay $325,000 and if they were to resume hiring efforts in the US, they are required to call back all applicants during the April-May 2020 period who were rejected based on age to re-apply.

Prior to this settlement, the EEOC issued guidance to employers about their use of Artificial Intelligence tools that extends existing employer selection guidelines to AI-assisted selections. [From this guidance, employers, not third-party vendors, ultimately bear the risk of unintended adverse discrimination from such tools.](https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2024/03/navigating-ai-in-the-workplace-and-recruitment)

**How Do HR AI Solutions Introduce Bias?**

There are several steps in the job search process and AI is integrated throughout this process. Steps include: The initial search, narrowing candidates, and screening.

***Initial search***

The job search process starts with targeted ads reaching the right people. Algorithms in hiring can steer job ads towards specific candidates and help assess their competencies using new and novel data. [HR professionals found the tool helpful](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0) in drafting precise language and designing the ad with position elements, content and requirements. [But these platforms can inadvertently reinforce gender and racial stereotypes by delivering the ad to candidates that meet certain job stereotypes.](https://hbr.org/2019/05/all-the-ways-hiring-algorithms-can-introduce-bias)

For instance, [ads delivered on Facebook for stereotypically male jobs are overwhelmingly targeted at male users even though the advertising was intended to reach a gender neutral audience.](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0) Essentially, at this step of the job search process, algorithms can prevent capable candidates from even seeing the job posting in the first place that further creates a barrier to employment.

***Narrowing Candidates***

After candidates that have viewed and applied for the job through an ad or other source, the next step that AI streamlines is the candidate narrowing process. At this step, the system narrows candidates by reviewing resumes that best match the historical hiring data from the company or its training data. [Applicants found the resume to application form data transfers helpful and accurate in this step of the process. But applicants were concerned that the model could miss necessary information.](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0)

From the company’s perspective, hiring practices from the client company are still incorporated into the hiring criteria in the licensed model. [While the algorithm is helpful in parsing vast amounts of resumes and streaming this laborious process for professionals, the algorithm can replicate and amplify existing biases in the company data.](https://hbr.org/2019/05/all-the-ways-hiring-algorithms-can-introduce-bias)

For example, a manager’s past decisions may lead to anchoring bias. [If some bias like gender, education, race and age existed in the past and they are present in the employer’s current high performing employees that the company uses as a benchmark, those biases can be incorporated into the outcomes at this stage of the em](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0)ployment search process.

***Screening***

Some organizations subscribe to AI tools that have a computer vision-powered virtual interview process that analyzes the candidates’ expressions to determine whether they fit the “ideal candidate” profile, while other tools like behavior/skills games are used to screen candidates prior to an in-person interview.

Computer vision models that analyze candidate expressions to assess candidacy are found to perpetuate preexisting biases against people of color. [For instance, a study that evaluates such tools, found the taxonomies](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13347-022-00543-1) of social and behavioral components creates and sustains similar biased observations that one human would make on an another because the model with those labels and taxonomies is trained with power hierarchies. In this sense, computer vision AI hiring tools are not neutral because they reflect the humans that train and rely on them.

Similarly, skill games are another popular tool used to screen candidates. However, there are some relationships AI cannot perceive in its analysis. [For instance, candidates that are not adept with online games perform poorly on those games, not because they lack the skills, but they lack an understanding of the games features.](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0) Algorithms, while trained on vast data to assess candidate ability, still fall short when it comes assessing general human capabilities like the relationship between online game experience and employment skills tests.

Throughout each step of the employment search process, AI tools fall short in accurately capturing candidate potential capabilities.

**Discrimination Theories and AI**

Given that the potential for bias is embedded throughout the employment search process, legal scholars speculate courts are more likely to scrutinize discriminatory outcomes under the [disparate impact theory of discrimination.](https://www.eeoc.gov/sites/default/files/2024-04/20240429_What%20is%20the%20EEOCs%20role%20in%20AI.pdf)

As a recap, under Title VII there are two theories of discrimination, disparate treatment, and disparate impact. Disparate treatment means the person is treated different “because of” their status as a protected class (i.e., race, sex). For example, if a manager were to intentionally use a bias algorithm to screen out candidates of a certain race, then this behavior would be considered disparate treatment. Note, this scenario is for illustrative purposes only.

Disparate impact applies to facially neutral processes that have a discriminatory effect. The discriminatory effect aspect of this theory of discrimination can be complex because the plaintiff must identify the employer practice that has a disparate impact on a protected group. The employer can then defend that the practice by showing it is “job related” and consistent with “business necessity.” However, the plaintiff can still show that there was an alternative selection process and the business failed to adopt it. Based on this disparate impact theory, it is possible that when AI selection tools disproportionately screen women and/or racial minorities from the applicant pool, disparate theory could apply.

**Existing Methods to Mitigate Bias**

Algorithmic bias in AI tools has serious implications for members of protected classes.

However, developers currently employ various tools to de-bias algorithms and improve their accuracy. [One method is de-biased word embedding in which neutral associations of a word are supplemented to expand the model’s understanding of the word. For instance, a common stereotype is men are doctors and women](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3611313) are nurses or in algorithmic terms “doctor – man + woman = nurse.” However, with the de-bias word embedding process, the model is then trained to understand “doctor – man + woman = doctor.”

Another practice currently employed by OpenAI is [external Red Teaming.](https://cdn.openai.com/papers/openais-approach-to-external-red-teaming.pdf) In which external stakeholders interact with the product and assess its weaknesses, possibility for bias, or other adverse consequences and provide feedback to OpenAI to improve and mitigate the onsets of adverse events.

But there are limitations to these enhancements. To start, bias mitigation is not a one-size-fits-all issue. [Bias is specific to its geographic and cultural bounds.](https://openai.com/index/how-should-ai-systems-behave/#:~:text=We%20believe%20that%20AI%20should%20be%20a%20useful%20tool%20for%20individual%20people%2C%20and%20thus%20customizable%20by%20each%20user%20up%20to%20limits%20defined%20by%20society.%20Therefore%2C%20we%20are%20developing%20an%20upgrade%20to%20ChatGPT%20to%20allow%20users%20to%20easily%20customize%20its%C2%A0behavior.) For instance, a model in India may need to consider caste-based discrimination. Additionally, precision is required to capture the frame where bias is possible and solely relying on foreseeable bias from the developers’ perspective is limiting. [Rather, employing some form of collaborative design that includes relevant stakeholders to contribute to the identification of bias, the identification of not biased is needed.](https://openai.com/index/how-should-ai-systems-behave/#:~:text=As%20a%20starting,is%20being%C2%A0deployed).)

Lastly, a debiased model is not a panacea. [A recent study in which users interacted with a debiased model that used machine learning and deep learning to provide recommendations for college majors,](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3611313) indicated that regardless of the debiased model’s output, users relied on their own biases to choose their majors, often motivated by gender stereotypes associated with those majors.

Essentially, solutions from the developer side are not enough to resolve algorithmic bias issues.

**Efforts to Regulate AI Employment Discrimination**

Federal law does not specifically govern artificial intelligence. However, existing laws including Title VII extend to applications that include AI. At this point, regulation efforts are largely at the state and local government level.

Additionally, the [EEOC’s Employer Guidelines is a start to shifting the onus on to employers to investigate the capabilities and outcomes of the technologies incorporated into their hiring practices.](https://www.eeoc.gov/sites/default/files/2024-04/20240429_What%20is%20the%20EEOCs%20role%20in%20AI.pdf)

[New York City is the first local government to pass an official law that regulates AI-empowered employment decision tools.](https://plusai.lexis.com/document?pddocfullpath=%2fshared%2fdocument%2fanalytical-materials%2furn%3acontentItem%3a66CG-RBB0-R03M-33BH-00000-00&pdmfid=1545874&pdcontentcomponentid=233714&pdproductcontenttypeid=urn:pct:24&pdisdoclinkaccess=true&pdsearchmode=chatbot_citation) The statute requires organizations to inform candidates of the use of AI in their hiring process, and before using the screening device, notify potential candidates. If candidates do not consent to the AI-based process, the organization is required to use an alternative method.

Like New York’s statute, [Connecticut passed a statute specific to state agency’s use of AI and machine learning hiring tools.](https://plusai.lexis.com/api/document?collection=statutes-legislation&id=urn:contentItem:6B9V-43P3-RWKW-C1WW-00000-00&context=1545874) Connecticut requires an annual review of the tools performance, a status update on whether the tool underwent some form of bias mitigation training in an effort to prevent unlawful discrimination.

[New Jersey](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/finance/ai-hiring-decisions), [California](https://legiscan.com/CA/text/AB2930/id/2930688), and [Washington D.C.](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/finance/ai-hiring-decisions) currently have bills that are intended to prevent discrimination with AI hiring systems.

**Employer Considerations**

With the possibility of bias embedded throughout each step of the recruiting process, employers must do their part to gather information about the performance of the AI system they ultimately invest in.

To start, recruiters and managers alike stressed the need for AI systems to [provide some explanation why the applicant is rejected or selected](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0) to accurately assess the performance of the model. This need speaks specifically to AI models’ tendency to find proxies or shortcuts in the data to reach the intended outcome on a superficial level. For instance, models might find a candidate by only focusing on candidates who graduated from universities in the Midwest if most of upper management attended such schools. In this sense, employers should look for accuracy reports, ask vendors ways to identify and correct this issue in this hiring pool.

Similarly, models can focus on candidate traits that are unrelated to the job traits and are simply unexplained correlations. [For example, one model in the UK linked people that liked “curly fries” on Facebook to have higher levels of intelligence.](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10111-022-00716-0) In this case, employers need to develop processes to analyze whether the output from the model was “job related” or related carrying out the functions of the business.

Lastly, employers must continue to invest in robust diversity training. Algorithmic bias reflects the bias human behind the computer. While AI tools enhance productivity and alleviate the laborious parts of work, it also increases the pressure on humans to do more cognitive-intensive work. In this sense, managers need robust diversity training to scrutinize outputs from AI models, to investigate whether the model measured what it was supposed to, whether the skills required in the post accurately reflect the expectations and culture of the organization.

Along with robust managerial training, these AI solutions often incorporate “culture fit” as a criterion. Leaders need to be intentional about precisely defining culture and promoting that defined culture in its hiring practices.

A machine does not know its output is biased. Humans interact with context—culture dictates norms and expectations, shared social/cultural history informs bias. Humans, whether we like to admit it or not, know when our output is biased.

To effectively mitigate unintentional bias in AI-driven hiring, stakeholders, ranging from HR professionals to developers and candidates, must understand the technology’s limitations, ensure its job-related decision-making accuracy, and promote transparent, informed use, while also maintaining robust DEI initiatives and awareness of candidates’ rights.

--- SOURCE 2: Opening the 'black box' of HRM algorithmic biases – How hiring ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213

SUMMARY:
Online freelancing platforms depend greatly on algorithms and artificial intelligence (AI) in implementing HRM practices (Kellogg et al., 2020) as these techniques create, for instance, rankings of potential candidates, which clients use to identify, compare, and hire suitable candidates for specific job offers (Bucher et al., 2021). To shed light on this algorithmic “black box” (Köchling &Wehner, 2020), this study responds to the call made by several previous studies for reducing AI-induced bias (Budhwar et al., 2022) by investigating the algorithmic mechanisms that induce discrimination on freelancing platforms. Third, other than previous studies analyzing discrimination on freelancing platforms based on subjective user data, this study relies on a large dataset of freelancer profiles and clients’ real selection decisions by considering the number of jobs as the mediating variable (Hannák et al., 2017).

FULL CONTENT:
1. Introduction
---------------
Recruiting freelancers has become an increasingly relevant HR strategy for temporarily scaling an organization’s workforce to absorb workload peaks or bridge gaps ([Hatfield & Lougee, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0190)). The growth of the gig economy is an observable trend worldwide. Recent statistics approximate the annual growth of the gig labor force since 2020 to be more than 500% in Asian countries such as Japan, and more than 300% in European countries like Spain and the UK. The US gig economy shows the same trend as it is growing more than eight times faster than the US economy as a whole ([Kulach, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0290)). Within the gig economy, freelancing platforms (e.g., Upwork) act as intermediaries between self-employed freelancers and clients (mainly companies’ HR departments) with the key purpose of matching the supply of and demand for labor. It is thus a triadic relationship ([Jackson et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0215)) in which intermediary platform firms are instrumental in important HRM processes, such as selection and workforce planning ([Meijerink & Keegan, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0355)).
Online freelancing platforms depend greatly on algorithms and artificial intelligence (AI) in implementing HRM practices ([Kellogg et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0260)) as these techniques create, for instance, rankings of potential candidates, which clients use to identify, compare, and hire suitable candidates for specific job offers ([Bucher et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0045)). Algorithmic HRM decision-making is becoming increasingly common ([Budhwar et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0050), [Meijerink et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0350)). Organizations apply algorithmic decision-making with the aim of enhancing the objectivity of processes ([Köchling & Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270)). Hence, platform providers are often accredited with a neutral role in the online labor market ([Fieseler et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0130)). This neutrality is often reinforced by the anonymity of the user profiles. Many platforms, such as Upwork or TaskRabbit, do not ask about freelancers’ demographics. Although freelancing platforms tend to offer an equal and discrimination-free labor marketplace, there are mounting complaints from freelancers feeling unfairly treated and discriminated by platforms’ algorithms ([Ferrari and Graham, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0125), [Jarrahi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0235), [Lee et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0310)). Evidence indicates that discrimination based on gender and race remains persistent for gig economy workers ([Chen et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0075), [Hannák et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185)). Freelancing platforms especially use algorithms to create rankings of potential candidates in response to queries from recruiters ([Bucher et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0045)). Such algorithm-based rankings play an important role in the online context in general. Many studies have shown that online platform users are much more likely to select higher-ranked items ([Craswell et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0085), [Guan and Cutrell, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0175)). This behavior has even been observed to be stable in studies where users were presented a reversed list of Google search results, and they still predominantly clicked on the items at the top of the list, even though these were actually the worst options ([Keane et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0250)). Hence, ranking algorithms that systematically privilege or discriminate against candidates with certain demographic characteristics reproduce real-world social inequalities within online labor marketplaces ([Chen, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0080)).
Recognizing discrimination issues in the use of AI and algorithmic HRM, efforts have been made to gain an understanding of the underlying reasons for these “algorithmic biases” ([Kordzadeh & Ghasemaghaei, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0275)). An analysis of the existing literature on the dark side of AI and algorithmic HRM uncovers two main sources of bias in algorithmic decision-making. The first source is an engineered bias ([O'Neil, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0400)). In this case, the algorithm would deliberately process critical demographic data to benefit or disadvantage certain individuals or groups compared to others. The second source is the quality of input data ([Köchling &Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270)). If the input dataset is biased, the algorithm will inevitably replicate this preexisting bias in its output ([Lindebaum et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0330)). Scholars from different disciplines have initiated a discourse on the dark side of AI and algorithmic HRM in the context of digital labor platforms ([Ferrari &Graham, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0125)). Yet it remains unclear how a platform’s algorithms induce biased rankings ([Chen et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0075), [Hannák et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185)) since platform providers commonly not disclose information on the algorithms used.
To shed light on this algorithmic “black box” ([Köchling &Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270)), this study responds to the call made by several previous studies for reducing AI-induced bias ([Budhwar et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0050)) by investigating the algorithmic mechanisms that induce discrimination on freelancing platforms. Drawing from the theory of statistical discrimination ([Zschirnt & Ruedin, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0535)) and the social dominance theory ([Sidanius & Pratto, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0495)), we investigate the mediating role of clients’ selection decisions using a sample of 44,167 freelancer profiles from Upwork, one of the world’s largest online freelancing platforms. Using structural equation modeling, our study uncovers an indirect algorithmic discrimination mechanism explaining how algorithm-based rankings disadvantage freelancers based on the three most predominant demographic characteristics of gender, race, and age. To obtain such demographic data, we utilize a novel AI-based picture analysis approach on the freelancers’ profile pictures. Our findings are theoretically important in several ways. First, we enhance the research on algorithmic HRM by uncovering a mechanism for ranking algorithms that demonstrably induces discrimination based on race, gender, and age by replicating human selection bias. Thus, we contribute to the literature on the dark side of AI and algorithmic HRM in helping to develop an understanding of why algorithms in HRM fail to counteract the problems they were designed to solve, like racial or gender bias and inequality ([Bankins et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0010), [Giermindl et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0150)). Second, our study contributes to the discrimination literature as one of the first studies to address the gap in empirical research on age discrimination in the online context ([Rosales & Fernández-Ardèvol, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0445)). By identifying a significant curvilinear effect up to the age of the early 40’s, our study provides evidence that younger candidates face similar challenges entering the freelancing market than the traditional labor market, thus hinting towards an age discrimination issue in the gig economy. Third, other than previous studies analyzing discrimination on freelancing platforms based on subjective user data, this study relies on a large dataset of freelancer profiles and clients’ real selection decisions by considering the number of jobs as the mediating variable ([Hannák et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185)). Consequently, the findings of this study capture the challenges of discrimination in the online labor market with high external validity, incorporating not only conscious decisions but also clients’ unconscious patterns in the selection process. Fourth, we advance the existing HRM literature by providing and validating an AI-based methodology to automatically identify gender, race, and age based on a profile picture. Thus, this study leverages recent advancements in the field of image recognition.
2. Conceptual background
------------------------
### 2.1. Algorithmic HRM practices on freelancing platforms
AI-based technologies, which rely upon algorithms, are inherently changing the way companies work (e.g., [Budhwar et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0050), [Cameron et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0060), [Duggan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0100), [Jarrahi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0230), [Malik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0335)). As they forge their way into HR practices, AI-based technologies can be leveraged in different HR functions, such as to support recruitment decisions, payroll management, or employee performance evaluations ([Budhwar et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0050), [Malik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0335)).
This technological infusion of HRM is summarized in the concept of“algorithmic HRM”, which we define as the use of algorithmic systems that rely on machine-readable data and automated data processing to assist decision-making processes in HR practices ([Duggan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0100), [Malik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0335), [Meijerink et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0350), [Meijerink and Bondarouk, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0345)).
Algorithmic HRM research has recently identified the gig economy as an increasingly relevant labor market phenomenon. Within the gig economy, a work relationship is defined as an “indirect relationship involving a minimum of three parties: intermediary online platform, worker and customer” ([Duggan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0100), p.117), where customers (what we call clients) are often companies. Work relationships in the gig economy are classified into three types: capital platform work (e.g., on Airbnb and Etsy), crowdwork (what we call freelancing on platforms such as Upwork, Fiverr, or TaskRabbit), and app-work (e.g., on Uber, Lyft, or Deliveroo) ([Duggan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0100)). Research on algorithmic HRM within the gig economy has especially begun to focus on freelancing (e.g., [Rahman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0430), [Waldkirch et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0515)) and app-work (e.g., [Duggan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0100), [Duggan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0095), [Ferrari and Graham, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0125), [Möhlmann et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0360)). The focus of this paper is on freelancing platforms acting as intermediaries within work relationships by supporting algorithmic HRM decision-making. Online freelancing platforms heavily rely on algorithms ([Blyth et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0040), [Bucher et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0045), [Jarrahi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0235), [Kellogg et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0260), [Rahman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0430), [Sühr et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0505)), for instance, to create rankings of freelancers, manage transactions, solve conflicts on the platform, or monitor the platform’s safety, thus providing assistance in HR decision-making by identifying and comparing suitable freelancers ([Bucher et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0045), [Jarrahi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0235), [Rahman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0430), [Sühr et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0505)). As the rankings on these platforms are especially central for freelancers’ “livelihood, it is critical to ensure that the underlying algorithms are not adversely affecting underrepresented groups” ([Sühr et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0505), p.989). Hence, this study investigates algorithmic bias on freelancing platforms.
### 2.2. Algorithmic bias on freelancing platforms
Algorithmic bias can initiate discrimination against several stakeholders, making it one of the major challenges accompanying the introduction of AI-based technologies ([Kordzadeh &Ghasemaghaei, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0275)). While numerous definitions of the term “algorithmic bias” exist, mainly due to the different philosophical, legal, and social perspectives on equality, [Kordzadeh and Ghasemaghaei (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0275) provide an overarching definition of algorithmic bias as “a systematic deviation from equality that emerges in the outputs of an algorithm” (p. 395). The emergence of an algorithmic bias can, when bias adjustments are not successfully performed, yield a benefit or disadvantage for a certain group or individuals compared to others, thus inducing discrimination ([Köchling and Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270), [Munn, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0365), [Zechiel et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0530)).
Literature presents various taxonomies and typologies of reasons for discrimination through algorithmic bias, often in specific contexts. Research on algorithmic HRM decision-making processes has proposed two main reasons for algorithmic bias. The first reason refers to the programming and engineering of algorithms. In this regard, a so-called “engineered bias” can occur if the algorithm deliberately processes critical demographic data to benefit or disadvantage certain individuals or groups compared to others ([O'Neil, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0400)). Furthermore, a bias can result from technical constraints (“technical bias”), such as limited hardware and software capacities or limited programming skills ([Friedman and Nissenbaum, 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0140), [Köchling and Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270)). Lastly, algorithms are often based on a distinct logic (e.g., social or economic logic) and can exhibit biases if this underlying logic changes or becomes outdated, thus resulting in misdirected outputs ([Friedman and Nissenbaum, 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0140), [Köchling and Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270)).
The second potential reason for algorithmic bias is the quality of input data used to train the algorithm ([Köchling &Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270))​. Most algorithms learn from historical data ([Barocas & Selbst, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0015)), which might be biased in terms of current or future decisions. Another main factor is the overrepresentation or underrepresentation of certain groups or characteristics within the input data, which might result in discriminatory algorithmic outcomes—so-called “representation bias” ([Barocas &Selbst, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0015)). The literature presents several bias adjustment techniques for the identification and resolution of biases in the input data (e.g., [Shahbazi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0475)). Yet, if the training dataset is biased and no bias adjustments are successfully performed, the algorithm could replicate discrimination in its output ([Lindebaum et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0330)).
The investigation of discriminatory outcomes in algorithmic HRM has recently begun within the gig economy context. Research in app-work arrangements has shown, for instance, that Uber rides from drivers with African-American-sounding names were canceled more frequently than rides from drivers with White-sounding names ([Ge et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0145)). This study examines freelancing platforms, such as Upwork and TaskRabbit, which aim to create a more neutral environment by not explicitly displaying freelancers' demographic information. Yet freelancers complain of receiving unfair treatment and discrimination from the platforms’ algorithms ([Jarrahi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0235), [Rahman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0430), [Waldkirch et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0515), [Wood et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0525)). One difficulty in investigating discrimination on freelancing platforms is that demographic information is—for good reasons—not displayed on these platforms. Nevertheless, discrimination appears persistent, which might be caused by perceived information, i.e., as the freelancers’ (first) names and pictures are available, they might be prone to get discriminated against their perceived gender, race, or age through, for instance, “stereotyping or subconscious preconceptions” ([Hannák et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185), p.1915). However, empirical studies on this issue are notably scarce. [Hannák et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185) produced the first and, to date, only research to overcome methodological issues by collecting 13,500 freelancers’ profiles on the two online freelancing platforms TaskRabbit and Fiverr and manually classifying the freelancers’ perceived gender and race. They found that gender and race significantly correlated with the amount and nature of social feedback. For instance, Black freelancers received worse ratings than similar White freelancers. In addition, women received fewer reviews than men on TaskRabbit. They also found a significant correlation between the freelancers’ perceived race and gender and their ranking position within search results. In summary, their paper reveals various discriminatory outcomes about gender and race on these two freelancing platforms yet issues a clear call to investigate the causes of this discrimination further ([Hannák et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185)).
A central explanation for discrimination on freelancing platforms—that is, advantaging or disadvantaging individuals with certain characteristics within a ranking—could be attributed to algorithmic bias in the ranking’s creation. These platforms leverage algorithms to create rankings, thus supporting clients in identifying and comparing potential freelancers for specific job offers ([Bucher et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0045), [Jarrahi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0235), [Rahman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0430), [Sühr et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0505)). Although research has found for example biased rankings to the detriment of Black relative to White, it remains unclear how a platform’s algorithms create such biased rankings ([Chen et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0075), [Hannák et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0185)).
While it neither appears plausible that freelancing platforms intentionally design their ranking algorithms in a discriminatory way nor that the platforms lack the technical capacity to create fair rankings, biased rankings could be rooted in the input data. In the traditional labor context, there is extensive research on hiring discrimination demonstrating biased selection decisions based on gender, race, and age (e.g., [Bertrand and Mullainathan, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0025), [Neumark et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0375)). Yet, within the freelancing context, clients’ hiring decisions could be affected by the perceived gender, race, and age associated with profile pictures, leading to a reproduction of these selection decisions in the rankings of the freelancing platform.
Studies on the traditional labor market have analyzed discrimination based on demographics, such as gender, race, and age. First, concerning gender, research has predominantly identified discrimination against women in hiring decisions (e.g., [Bertrand and Mullainathan, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0025), [Goldin and Rouse, 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0160), [Neumark et al., 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0370), [Olian et al., 1988](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0395)). Yet, more recent research has begun to present contrasting results (e.g., [Albert et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0005), [Birkelund et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0035), [Leung et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0320)). Second, there is a wealth of literature on discrimination based on race. For example, research highlights that especially Black candidates have lower chances of getting hired (e.g., [Bertrand and Mullainathan, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0025), [Jacquemet and Yannelis, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0220), [Kushins, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0295), [Leung et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0320), [Quillian et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0420), [Quillian et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0425)). Lastly, age can play a role in HR hiring decisions. Research mainly reveals that older candidates face discrimination (e.g., [Albert et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0005), [Lahey, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0300), [Neumark et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0375), [Neumark et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0380)).
These results offer important insights into human hiring decisions. Yet, there is no research applying this knowledge to the analysis of algorithmic bias in the rankings of freelancing platforms. Hence, in this paper, we shed light on the complexity of biased rankings on freelancing platforms by investigating the relationship between gender, race, and age and a platform’s ranking. To consider the full complexity of biased rankings, we especially focus on the mediating effects of human hiring decisions within the creation of biased rankings.
3. Hypothesis development
-------------------------
### 3.1. Ranking algorithms on freelancing platforms
The algorithmic logic of processing input data according to several criteria is key to analyzing potential algorithmic bias in the rankings of freelancing platforms. While it seems implausible that platforms’ algorithms would be deliberately engineered to use profile pictures to obtain demographic information and subsequently produce biased outcomes (i.e., engineered bias), the key to understanding algorithm-induced discrimination may be rooted in the choice and processing of input data ([Köchling &Wehner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0270)). A freelancer’s profile does not explicitly contain demographic information but different types of qualification- and job-related information. Qualification-related information includes objective information, such as level of education, and mainly subjective information, such as a self-reported skill set. The job-related information refers to different types of records from past jobs and information related to future jobs, like the hourly rate. Similar to qualification-related information, job records can be distinguished into subjective information, like content descriptions, reviews, and ratings by clients, and objective records, such as the total number of jobs and total earnings. While all this information might basically be processed by a platform’s ranking algorithm, analyses of rankings in other contexts show that objective variables, such as the number of sales on Amazon ([Sharma et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0480)) or the total number of citations on Google Scholar ([Martin-Martin et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0340), [Rovira et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0455)), significantly impact the ranking of items. For example, [Rovira et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0455) tested different search strategies using one or more search terms or applying restrictions in the search. In all cases, the correlations were higher than 0.7, suggesting that citation counts are likely the primary ranking factor with significant weight in the sorting algorithm. In line with these findings, we focus our analyses on the total number of jobs as an objective variable and its role in creating the platform’s ranking—that is, the platform’s ranking algorithm. If the total number of jobs or, rather, how many times freelancers have been hired by clients, is a key variable for the platform’s ranking, this means that the (perceived) discrimination reported by freelancers ([Jarrahi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0235), [Lee et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0148296325001213#b0310)) might be the result of an indirect relationship, mediated by the clients’ hiring decisions, with the total number of jobs being the mediating variable.

# Understanding Algorithmic Bias to Improve Talent Acquisition Outcomes

How to recognize and mitigate AI bias in talent acquisition. Ensure fair hiring practices while leveraging the benefits of AI for recruitment.

### Key Takeaways

The use of artificial intelligence (AI) in recruiting practices is on the rise. Recent surveys from IBM show that 19% of global enterprises already [use AI for talent acquisition](https://newsroom.ibm.com/2024-01-10-Data-Suggests-Growth-in-Enterprise-Adoption-of-AI-is-Due-to-Widespread-Deployment-by-Early-Adopters). Data from Gartner shows that 76% of recruiting professionals believe their [company will fall behind](https://www.gartner.com/en/human-resources/topics/artificial-intelligence-in-hr) if they don't implement AI soon and that as many as half plan to use it to write job descriptions, interview questions, and recruitment marketing materials.

While AI technology can create efficient hiring processes, it can just as easily lead to AI bias–results that reflect human biases–leading to inequalities in recruiting and talent acquisition.

## The Consequences of AI Bias in Recruitment

All hiring professionals need to pay attention to AI bias, which can have a significant negative impact in various ways.

### Legal Consequences of Discrimination

Though few federal laws currently address the issue of technology-related discrimination, employers may still be held responsible for the outcomes produced by AI technology under laws such as the Americans with Disabilities Act and Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission is monitoring algorithmic bias and has developed an Artificial Intelligence and Algorithmic Fairness Initiative to gather insight into these technologies' impacts and issue guidance on mitigating bias.

Until that happens, [several U.S. states and territories](https://iapp.org/resources/article/us-state-ai-governance-legislation-tracker/) have implemented laws. For example, New York's Automated Employment Decision Tools law requires local business owners to assess whether their AI technology negatively impacts protected groups and publish the results on their websites.

Employers hiring candidates worldwide should also understand the global landscape regarding laws governing the use of AI technology. For example, the EU has adopted the [Artificial Intelligence Act](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20240308IPR19015/artificial-intelligence-act-meps-adopt-landmark-law), which outright bans using biometric and facial recognition databases in hiring practices. Canada, China, and India have adopted similar laws or issued accountability guidance to employers.

### Impact on Diversity and Candidate Trust

AI bias can negatively impact DEI efforts. Suppose the technology is trained on data or uses an algorithm that presents biases against people from underrepresented groups; in that case, companies won't have the opportunity to interview those candidates, resulting in a less diverse workplace.

A lack of diversity resulting from algorithmic bias can have far-reaching effects on employee satisfaction and the employer brand. If candidates believe that a company employs discriminatory hiring practices, it can result in a lawsuit and a loss of trust, causing candidates to avoid the company altogether.

### Missing Out on Top Talent

Biased algorithms can lead to additional missed opportunities for top talent. If AI technology filters out qualified candidates based on an undetected bias, those candidates aren't reaching the talent pool and may be hired elsewhere.

![Understanding Algorithmic Bias to Improve Talent Acquisition Outcomes](https://info.recruitics.com/hs-fs/hubfs/iStock-1482915045.jpg?width=530&height=353&name=iStock-1482915045.jpg)

![Understanding Algorithmic Bias to Improve Talent Acquisition Outcomes](https://info.recruitics.com/hs-fs/hubfs/iStock-1482915045.jpg?width=530&height=353&name=iStock-1482915045.jpg)

## The Root Causes of Bias in Algorithms

Data trains AI algorithms to make automated decisions. The outcomes will likely be the same when those data samples are biased. For example, when training facial or voice recognition software, it's essential to use people from all racial and ethnic backgrounds. Otherwise, the software algorithm may inadvertently develop a bias toward those absent from the dataset.

Additionally, algorithms are programmed by human developers. If those developers carry conscious or unconscious biases, those thoughts can also show up in the results. Developers may accidentally give unfair advantages to specific groups by unfairly weighting certain factors or criteria in the selection process.

## Lessons From a Tech Giant

In 2018, Amazon discontinued its [AI-driven hiring technology](https://www.reuters.com/article/us-amazon-com-jobs-automation-insight-idUSKCN1MK08G/) after it was found to show bias against women. The algorithm was trained to select applicants based on resumes the company had received over the preceding decade. Because it had not received many applications from women, the computer models began to prefer male candidates and penalized resumes with the word "women."

Although this is only one example of AI bias's negative impact on hiring practices, it highlights the importance of employers doing the necessary work to ensure AI bias does not lead to unfair outcomes that harm candidates or the organization.

## Practical Strategies for Mitigating AI Bias

Studies show that 71% of U.S. candidates [oppose AI](https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2023/04/20/ai-in-hiring-and-evaluating-workers-what-americans-think/) in final hiring decisions, and 41% oppose its use in reviewing job applications. Hiring professionals also express concerns about AI's ability to produce accurate results. However, employing practical strategies to mitigate known issues with AI bias can help bridge the gap and promote trust across the board.

### Ensuring Human Oversight

One such strategy is ensuring human oversight of company processes involving AI algorithms. Doing so may allow hiring professionals to spot potential biases before they negatively impact candidates or the company.

### Vetting Vendor Practices

Employers should ensure that any software vendor they work with is committed to auditing and improving data quality and diversity, performing regular algorithm audits, and testing for fairness. They may even implement fairness-aware algorithms specifically programmed to account for biases.

### Considering Blind Recruitment

Talent acquisition professionals may also consider implementing blind recruitment policies, which require removing personal identifiers from resumes. If a particular software has been trained with a bias toward certain groups, removing this information may mitigate that inherent bias and allow for a more fair hiring process.

### Encouraging Candidate Feedback

Hiring professionals should always encourage candidates to provide feedback about their experience. If an employer notices that candidates from specific groups are having issues with the process, it may be necessary to determine whether technology is to blame. This, in turn, can help counteract the effects of AI bias and improve the hiring process for all candidates.

![Understanding Algorithmic Bias to Improve Talent Acquisition Outcomes](https://info.recruitics.com/hs-fs/hubfs/iStock-1430627448.jpg?width=530&height=353&name=iStock-1430627448.jpg)

![Understanding Algorithmic Bias to Improve Talent Acquisition Outcomes](https://info.recruitics.com/hs-fs/hubfs/iStock-1430627448.jpg?width=530&height=353&name=iStock-1430627448.jpg)

## Benefits of Mitigating AI Bias

Mitigating AI bias has many benefits for companies and candidates. Aside from reduced legal and compliance risk exposure, reducing bias enhances diversity and inclusion, leading to better employee satisfaction and engagement, and feelings of psychological safety and trust. Companies can experience a stronger employer brand and reduced turnover.

Additionally, removing AI bias improves hiring outcomes and gives hiring professionals access to previously untapped talent pools. When automated systems don't filter out qualified applicants from underrepresented groups, there are more high-quality candidates to choose from.

## Best Practices for Ensuring Fairness

## Balancing the Need for Fair Outcomes

Artificial intelligence enhances efficiency in the hiring process. However, talent acquisition professionals must be aware of potential bias and take steps to mitigate it to protect both candidates and the organization.

Doing so helps employers build trust with candidates and employees and ensures the organization reaps the benefits of a highly qualified and diverse workforce. Hiring professionals can rest assured that recognizing and thwarting biases can go a long way toward helping the organization fulfill its goals and objectives.
